summary: DHH reveals that HEY email service originally started as an attempt to build Highrise 2, a successor to 37signals' 2007 CRM product. After several failed skunkworks attempts, the team realized email is the true center of CRM and built HEY first without the CRM features. Now they're adding CRM-like capabilities back with HEY Workflows and Contact Notes. The goal is to serve small businesses that need 'email++' rather than competing with enterprise tools like Salesforce.
key_points: HEY was originally conceived as Highrise 2, not a general-purpose email service — email turned out to be the foundation of CRM
HEY Workflows replaces the need for dedicated SaaS tools for small-scale processes like hiring funnels or invoice tracking
37signals deliberately avoids building for enterprise needs, staying focused on small business use cases
The philosophy is 'just-right' software that sits between an address book (too little) and traditional CRM (too much)
money_quotes: It was realizing that email is the true center of the CRM that paved the path toward a real product.

No, it's not going to replace Salesforce for huge enterprise teams that need to calculate their pipeline value for the VP of Finance for next quarter. We don't know anything about that world (and would prefer to keep it like that!).
For every workflow, there's probably a dedicated SaaS solution that you could add to your monthly nut, but do you really need it?
If you only hire a couple of times per year, you don't need a dedicated hiring app.
Your address book doesn't do enough. Traditional CRM software tries to do too much.
Small business users who just need email++ to run their shop – not a ton of individual, expensive services.
key_insight: Email is the true center of CRM for small businesses, and most don't need dedicated SaaS tools when lightweight workflow features built into their email can do the job.
